California ADU due diligence Owner checklist

ADU Permit Due Diligence Checklist for California Owners

ADU questions often show up after the build feels “done”: refinance, sale prep, project takeover, inherited plans, or a new consultant trying to understand what is already in motion. This checklist stays general on purpose. It is designed to help you verify the record trail without pretending every California city handles ADUs the same way.

Request Permit History + Risk Report Browse California jurisdictions
1. Verify the jurisdiction first

Every ADU review starts with the right permit lane.

Before you look at ADU scope, make sure you know which city or county actually controls the property. This is basic, but it is still the fastest place for bad assumptions to enter the file.

  • Use the California jurisdiction hub as the starting index.
  • If the property is in Los Angeles, compare Los Angeles and Los Angeles County when routing is unclear.
  • If you already know the city, go directly to the relevant jurisdiction page before assuming your old notes still apply.
Why ADUs need special care

The visible improvement can be much easier to understand than the permit trail behind it.

That gap is where owners, buyers, and new consultants lose time. A useful due-diligence pass narrows the gap between the built condition, the known project story, and the public record you can actually support.

2. Compare the property to the record

Ask whether the ADU story feels larger than the permit history you can see.

Location and form

Detached, attached, garage-conversion, and interior conversion projects all create different research questions. Start by describing what exists now.

Timeline clues

When did planning, construction, or occupancy likely happen? Even rough timing helps you focus the permit search and the document request.

Support files

Gather the owner packet, listing material, plan set references, or contractor notes that explain how the ADU story has been told so far.

3. Build a simple permit checklist

Keep the review practical and repeatable.

  • Record the exact property address and any known permit numbers.
  • Save public permit descriptions, dates, and status wording that appear relevant.
  • Note whether the visible ADU scope feels aligned, thinner, or more complicated than the record.
  • Flag anything that would matter to a lender, buyer, insurer, or new project team.
4. Watch for transition risk

ADU issues often surface during handoff moments.

Refinancing, selling, taking over a property, or switching architects or builders is often when someone finally wants the permit history laid out clearly. That is the moment to stop relying on memory and gather one defensible record set.

5. Use city-specific pages when you need local context

California-wide guidance gets stronger when you pair it with a live jurisdiction page.

  • Los Angeles for city permit history and scope signals.
  • Sacramento for portal-first permit checks and status review.
  • Santa Monica for high-attention due-diligence review.
CTA

Need one ADU address summarized clearly?

A Permit History + Risk Report is the faster route when you already know the property and need a cleaner answer than the public record gives you on its own.

Request Permit History + Risk Report See sample report
FAQ

Common California ADU questions

Does this checklist replace city-specific ADU guidance?

No. It is meant to help you gather and evaluate records before you move into city-specific procedural detail.

What if I only know the property and not the permit number?

That is still workable. Start with the jurisdiction and address, then capture any permit identifiers that appear during the search.

When is a report more useful than a checklist?

When the property is already active in a transaction, handoff, or internal review and you need one summary instead of open browser tabs.
